issue_title: Wrong bootstrap column calculations
issue_body:
As per object, on Bootstrap's *col-xs* devices (expecially when running Chrome) there's a small overlap between these two big main divs, *#content* and *#sidebar-menu*. This is preventing both correct website usage and sidebar menu hiding (the show-hide-sidebar-menu button falls behind the sidebar-menu div and hence it's not visible).

I've noticed this is due to a wrong match between the number of columns assigned to sidebar div and the number of columns assigned to the content div when dealing with *col-xs*-sized devices. Here's the code:

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:68**
`<div id="sidebar-menu" class="col-xs-6 col-sm-3 col-md-2 sidebar-menu">`

**bootstrap_admin/templates/admin/base.html:83**
`<div id="content" class="{% if is_displaying_menu %}col-xs-9` [...]

As you can see, with col-xs-6 and col-xs-9 we're having a total of 16 columns here... A bit too many :)

I've fixed that by replacing the "9" with another "6", which as far as I have seen leads to the best result. Any combination with a total of 12 would of course work as well. In any case, we have to accordingly replace as well the value here, or the menu won't hide correctly:

**bootstrap_admin/static/bootstrap_admin/js/base.js:31**
`main_classes = 'col-xs-9 ` [...]

In my case, I've put a "6" there as well, as it has to match the one in *base.html:83*.

Hope this helps.
Cheers.

issue_title: Wrap DOT/PSA views in DRF views
issue_body:
Hi, me again 😄 

If I understand correctly, the aim of this package is to provide seamless integration of `django-oauth-toolkit` and `python-social-auth` with Django REST framework. This is why, I believe that this library should wrap around all the views that imports from DOT and PSA. 

Why is this important? Because by having all views inherit at least from DRF's `APIView` we allow the renderers and all other DRF related stuff kick in. For example I have a renderer that wraps all responses in `data` and adds `pagination` dict if necessary, but it will never kick in because DOT returns raw Django responses. Additionally this would allow this library to catch the raw errors coming from DOT/PSA/Oauthlib and have them go through DRF's exception handler -> return uniform error responses.

This would broaden the scope of the library, but I also believe would make it much more valuable. As there is no library out there which provides complete and seamless integration of OAuth with Django REST framework.

issue_title: format input data using mx.rnn
issue_body:
[data_ex_git.zip](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/files/1220978/data_ex_git.zip)
I've trying to forecast time-series using mx.rnn model but I can't shape the data to input  format asked from mx.rnn. I want do predict a variable Q_t using some past values from itself and another variables. Using mx.model.FeedForward.create is very easy to define. I can't understand the "labels" input on mx.rnn model. This is my code:

```
load("data_ex_git.RData") # attached


train <- data[1:dias_train,]
test <- data[(dias_train+1):nrow(data),]

# Neural net fitting

# Scaling data for the NN
maxs <- apply(data, 2, max)
mins <- apply(data, 2, min)
scaled <- as.data.frame(scale(data, center = mins, scale = maxs - mins))

train_ <- scaled[1:dias_train,]
test_ <- scaled[(dias_train+1):nrow(data),]

library(mxnet)
train.x <- data.matrix(train_[,-1])
train.y <- train_[,1]
test.x <- data.matrix(test_[,-1])
test.y <- test_[,1]

X.train <- list(data=t(train.x), label=t(train.y))
X.val <- list(data=t(test.x), label=t(test.y))

batch.size = 5
seq.len = 5
num.hidden = 3
num.embed = 3
num.rnn.layer = 1
num.lstm.layer = 1
num.round = 1
update.period = 1
learning.rate= 0.1
wd=0.00001
clip_gradient=1

mx.set.seed(0)
model <- mx.rnn(X.train, NULL, num.rnn.layer=num.rnn.layer, seq.len=seq.len, num.hidden=num.hidden,
                num.embed=num.embed, num.label=5, batch.size=batch.size, input.size=5, ctx = mx.cpu(),
                num.round = num.round, update.period = update.period, initializer = mx.init.uniform(0.01),
                dropout = 0, optimizer = "sgd", batch.norm = FALSE,
                learning.rate=learning.rate, wd=wd, clip_gradient=clip_gradient)

preds = predict(model,t(test.x))

issue_title: MXNet fcnxs examples: GPU memory usage varies during training.
issue_body:
When training the fcnxs examples, the GPU memory varies from 3GB to the MAX GPU Memory, which looks like a GPU memory leak problem. Did anybody get the same issue?
Also, how can we see the feature map when testing on an image? I mean, how can I see a specific layer's output without defining it as the output in the symbol file.

issue_title: Heteroscedastic dropout uncertainty
issue_body:
Can heteroscedastic dropout uncertainty be enabled in MXNet?

This follows from #3821 - I now have networks predicting with dropout uncertainty, but I believe this is homoscedastic uncertainty - see [Yarin Gal's blog post](https://github.com/yaringal/HeteroscedasticDropoutUncertainty).

Yarin provides an [example ConvnetJS code for heteroscedastic uncertainty](https://github.com/yaringal/HeteroscedasticDropoutUncertainty/blob/master/convnetjs/convnet_heteroscedastic.js#L971) but I don't know enough to apply this to MXNet myself.

Can anyone advise on how to implement heteroscedastic regression layers in MXNet? It seems this would be a very useful feature for all regression purposes.

Many thanks.

issue_title: A couple of issues in benchmark_score.py
issue_body:
Here are a couple of issues in incubator-mxnet/example/image-classification/benchmark_score.py.

Both prevent the script from running.

--> Issue #1
def get_symbol(network, batch_size):
    image_shape = (3,299,299) if network == 'inception-v3' else (3,224,224)
    num_layers = 0

get_symbol() for vgg16 doesn't accept a zero value for the number of layers.
Quick fix: insert this after "num_layers = 0"
if network=='vgg':
    num_layers=16
Or use the same solution as for resnet (split and extract number of layers)

--> Issue #2
devs = [mx.gpu(0)] if len(get_gpus()) > 0 else []

get_gpus() relies on nvidia-smi which is not installed on machines that don't have GPUs (say, c4 instances on AWS).

issue_title: Symbolic .json file not compatible with .params file generated since MXNet 1.2
issue_body:
`MXNet 1.2.0`

Reproducible here:

```
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon
net = gluon.nn.HybridSequential()
with net.name_scope():
    net.add(gluon.nn.Conv2D(10, (3, 3)))
    net.add(gluon.nn.Dense(50))
net.initialize()
net(mx.nd.ones((1,1,50,50)))
a = net(mx.sym.var('data'))
a.save('test.json')
net.save_params('test.params')
model = gluon.nn.SymbolBlock(outputs=mx.sym.load_json(open('test.json', 'r').read()),
                                 inputs=mx.sym.var('data'))
model.load_params('test.params', ctx=ctx)
```

Gives the following error:
```
AssertionError: Parameter 'conv0_weight' is missing in file 'test.params', which contains parameters: '0.weight', '0.bias', '1.weight', '1.bias'. Set allow_missing=True to ignore missing parameters.
```

This way to export symbol is recommended in [this tutorial on the straight dope](https://gluon.mxnet.io/chapter07_distributed-learning/hybridize.html)

issue_title: Better Validation-Accuracy with Tensorflow than MxNet
issue_body:
I tried to compare the speed and the validation accuracy of  a Convolutional Neural Network with 5 layers (3 convolutional layers and 2 fully connected layers) on MxNet and Tensorflow. For MNIST dataset , It shows that with the almost same accuracy, MxNet is quite faster than Tensorflow. However, I did this comparison with Dogs vs. Cats. It shows that MxNet cannot be as good as Tensorflow in accuracy. 
I have also tried Residual blocks, and again It demonstrated that the accuracy is not comparable with the accuracy that can be achieved by Tensorflow.

 I used mx.model.FeedForward.create for training, and mx.io.NDArrayIter for data traitors. I repeated the experiment with different sgd optimizer like Adam, sgd+momentom and RMSProp. However, couldn't achieve the same accuracy as Tensorflow.

Could you please help me to understand that where the problem might be from. Is it from Mxnet functions that I used or in general MxNet in not as accurate as Tensorflow?
Thank you in advance for your consideration.

issue_title: Add acceptance test for "accept unknown fields in response"
issue_body:
If the client receives extra data in the response, it should not raise an error.  A service should be able to add extra fields to its response object without having to do a api version bump, as long as the old fields are unmodified.

This has the potential to create a lot of failures. I don't believe there is a test for this in `swagger-py`, but we should verify it works correct in `bravado` before release.

issue_title: Django 1.8+: null_techical_500_response() needs to accept status_code kwarg
issue_body:
Found by:
 - Running Django 1.8+
 - Running latest django-extensions
 - Running runserver_plus
 - running in DEBUG=True
 - raising SuspiciousOperation exception in a view

...in the above flow of events, django's base handler picks up the SuspiciousOperation, calls through to debug.technical_500_response() passing a status_code kwarg of 400.

In runserver_plus, it appears django-extensions has patched out this function in favour of null_technical_500_response() to produce the nicer error pages.

null_technical_500_response() however crashes since it's not expecting a status_code kwarg.

In Django 1.8+, null_technical_500_response needs it's interface updating to reflect updates in Django 1.8 error handling.

issue_title: DB api improvements proposals
issue_body:
# DB api improvements proposals

This issue gather retrace the ideas we talked about during the Costa-Brava sprint.

## Transactions

### Simple transactions

Simplify the transaction opening api. It should always be a one line operation.

```python
txn = await tm.begin()
await txn.commit()
```

If the tm is associated with a request, the it should be passed to the
transaction, which removes any need to explicitely pass the request.

### Request default transaction

It the transaction is to become the default transaction of a request, the
eventual container of the request should be moved to the new transaction,
or refetched, and the transaction be set as the request.\_txn:

```python
txn = await request.begin()
await txn.commit()
```

Also, if the transaction is the default request transaction, commiting it
should call `execute_futures()` automatically.

### Context manager

A transaction manager for handling transactions could be provided (it
might already be the case):

```python
async with transaction(tm):
    pass
```


## Flush

In some cases it is needed to apply all changes to the database without closing
the transactions. There is currently no way to do that with the db api.

The proposal is to add a `flush()` function to Transaction, that does what
tpc_commit does (except for commit itself of course). This function would be
called automatically on commit.

```python
async txn.flush()
```


## Path-based index

### Problems

When a object is added, or its path changed, the navigation api (async_get, 
navigate_to) will not 'see' it until the changes are flushed to the database.

Implementing flush somehow fix the problem but at a double cost:

- the user must remember to flush before navigating
- a same object my be loaded/modified/flushed many times in a single transaction.

Another problem with the current implementation is that a same object can be
loaded several times as different instances, and loose all consistency between
those instances.

### Proposal

The idea is to add a path-based index of all the objects that were loaded.

This index would be used by the content api (async_get etc.), and achieve
complete consistency of the objects loaded in a transaction.

The index would use weakref so that the memory footprint is as low as possible
(we don't want to keep an object loaded when its not used anywhere).

issue_title: Contextualizing the event portlet even when Inherited
issue_body:
## Current Implementation

- Upon adding an event portlet, the user can select a folder or collection as the search base for events, otherwise, it will use the entire site as the search base. 
- However, when a subfolder, inherits the event portlet, the search base is the same as the parent portlet. 

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1830494/37123350-e8bedd4c-2231-11e8-8c3e-bf94b4048efa.png)


## Limitations

Subfolders' event portlet isn't contextualized, whereby the search base becomes the contexted or current folder automatically.  For example, on a Plone site, there's a committees page, with an event portlet that shows the top 5 upcoming events from all committee page and each committee page has an event portlet with their own events. 
With the current implementation, the site manager has to set the search base for each committee manually,  instead of automatically inheriting the event portlet with an option or value to set the search base to the current folder or context.

## Possible Solutions

- Add an optional checkbox field that will set the current context or folder has the search base.
- Change the search base from UUID to PATH search.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1830494/37123424-29f7ff82-2232-11e8-8989-b32b61487e56.png)

issue_title: pyzmq NOBLOCK recv() quite slow due to exception overhead
issue_body:
Hey, when zmq.Socket.recv() (and recv_multipart(), etc) is used in non blocking mode (flags=NOBLOCK), failure to read a message results in an exception being thrown (zmq.error.Again).

This is a little funny/nonpythonic because this isn't really an error condition, it's actually a very nominal condition with non-blocking I/O.

More significantly, however, throwing that exception is quite costly. On the Raspberry Pi I'm working on, for example, it ends up taking north of one millisecond to construct that exception, populate its stack trace (which means stat()'ing the backend module, etc), and generally perform related bookkeeping -- even though I'm just going to catch & throw away that exception and return to a polling loop.

That means if I have a 10msec polling loop (as part of a control system), and it checks zmq every time through that loop, pyzmq ends up burning *over 10% of the CPU* even when no messages are actually arriving.

It would be strongly desired to have some flag in the API which would allow non-blocking receive without throwing an exception when no messages are pending. (Or, equivalently, check to see if any messages are pending without performing an actual receive, or throwing an exception.)

issue_title: Introduce seperate batch sizes for train, validation, and test
issue_body:
The distribution of wav lengths in the train, validation, and test sets may be skewed. For example, if small wav files are in the training set and larger ones in the validation set.

If this is the case, then the maximum training batch size is too large for the validation set.

To solve this separate batch sizes should be introduced for the train, validation, and test sets.

Far from being theoretic, this skew exists in the TED data set. The largest wav files form the train, validation, and test sets are as follows:
```
train
-rw-rw-r-- 1 alissy alissy 634K Oct 10 05:47 JonathanHarris_2007P-391.71-411.97.wav

validation
-rw-rw-r-- 1 alissy alissy 1.3M Oct 10 05:47 WadeDavis_2003-123.31-163.23.wav

test
-rw-rw-r-- 1 alissy alissy 1018K Oct 10 05:50 JaneMcGonigal_2010-196.922-229.47.wav
```
As one case see, the largest wav in the validation data set is about twice the size of the largest wav in the test data set. A similar statement is true for the test data set. This causes the batch size 48 to work for the training set and fail for the validation set.

issue_title: Non-matplotlib figure objects need more than savefig
issue_body:
I have been using pytest-mpl to test the [gmt-python](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python) library. We have a `Figure` object that implements `savefig` and the tests were working fine in Python 3.5 and 3.6 ([an example test script](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/blob/master/gmt/tests/test_pscoast.py)).

A few weeks ago, our tests for 3.5 started failing ([example error log](https://travis-ci.org/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/jobs/275310274#L1930)). I reproduced the failing tests locally using a 3.5 version of our [default conda environment](https://github.com/GenericMappingTools/gmt-python/blob/master/environment.yml). I managed to track it down to `plt.close` not recognizing my `Figure` class as a valid figure object (see [the pyplot.close code](https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/master/lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py#L646)). 

The real mystery is that the same test with the same matplotlib version (2.0.2) passes on 3.6. 

Is there any way that pytest-mpl could avoid the call to `plt.close(fig)`? Even just removing the `fig` argument to close the current figure would already solve the problem. I can resolve this by introducing a `int` attribute on my `Figure` but that is very ugly.

If there is not way around it, then it would be good to include in the docs the need to implement an `int` attribute to fool `plt.close`. 

Otherwise, I'm very impressed that this worked so flawlessly! Congratulations!

issue_title: Inconsistent filename references to LocalTarget and MockTarget
OBJ Exporter can't correctly export vertex colors
issue_body:
What: For example exporting 25 different vertices, all with color 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0 results in having only one vertex in the resulting obj file. Having 10 different colors will result in only exporting 10 vertices.
Why: Internally the OBJ exporter uses a std::map to avoid exporting the same vertex twice. When adding color information to vertices, the exporter does not include color information as additional keys in the first map, but creates a second map for colors.
Thus, 2 different colors of 10 vertices leads to a color map with 2 elements.
Unfortunately while exporting all distinct vertices the color map is assumed to have the same amount of elements than the vertex map and a linear index is used to access them. If the index gets greater than the color map size, additional vertices are simply omited.
I will probably submit a PR in the next few days to fix this. If I do not, I hope this description helps!

issue_title: Python 2.5 does not support the tuple.index() method
issue_body:
The BitField does not seem to support Python 2.5 because the tuple.index() method was not introduced until 2.6.  If this is intended, it would be good to add a Python 2.6 requirement to the readme.  Otherwise, here's the error message that pops up whenever __ getattr __, __ setattr __, and __ contains __ are used for BitHandler:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<console>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/.../bitfield/**init**.py", line 217, in iteritems
    yield (k, getattr(self, k).is_set)
  File "/.../bitfield/**init**.py", line 178, in **getattr**
    return self.get_bit(self._keys.index(key))
AttributeError: 'tuple' object has no attribute 'index'

I was able to solve this by turning "self._keys.index(key)" to "list(self._keys).index(key)", but I'm not sure if this is the best approach.  I just wanted you to be aware of the tuple.index() issue in case you wanted to support Python 2.5.

issue_title: Endless migrations creation
issue_body:
Each time when execute `python manage.py makemigrations` it will create new migration:

```
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0015_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0016_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
(qb_celery) python manage.py makemigrations
Migrations for 'django_celery_beat':
  /home/user/.virtualenvs/qb_celery/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django_celery_beat/migrations/0017_auto_20171101_1607.py
    - Alter field event on solarschedule
```
The reason for that:

`SOLAR_SCHEDULES = [(x, _(x)) for x in schedules.solar._all_events]`

all_events:
```
_all_events = {
        'dawn_astronomical',
        'dawn_nautical',
        'dawn_civil',
        'sunrise',
        'solar_noon',
        'sunset',
        'dusk_civil',
        'dusk_nautical',
        'dusk_astronomical',
    }
```
so each time, when we import `from celery import schedules` it gives for SOLAR_SCHEDULES dict with different order.

issue_title: S3 bucket creation/deletion/waiter is inconsistent
issue_body:
I am running into several problems when creating/deleting buckets in S3 using boto3.

I have caught one of these problems involving the deletion of a bucket. The output is from py.test and clearly shows that the assert's value changed from false to true after the waiter had a chance to return.

```
    def delete(self):
        """ Deletes the bucket """
        try:
            self.bucket.load()
            self.bucket.delete()
        except:
            pass
        # sleep(1)
        self._bucket = s3.Bucket(self.name)
        self.bucket.wait_until_not_exists()
>       assert not self.exists
E       AssertionError: (assertion failed, but when it was re-run for printing intermediate values, it did not fail.  Suggestions: compute assert expression before the assert or use --assert=plain)
```

`self.bucket` is a s3.Bucket().

Here's the `exists` code:

```
    @property
    def exists(self):
        try:
            s3.meta.client.head_bucket(Bucket=self.name)
            return True
        except ClientError as e:
            # If a client error is thrown, then check that it was a 404 error.
            # If it was a 404 error, then the bucket does not exist.
            error_code = int(e.response['Error']['Code'])
            if error_code == 404:
                return False
```

I also have the issue on creation. The following code have many extra measures to workaround the bug and i still get failures from time to time!!

```
    def create(self):
        """ Creates the bucket """
        try:
            if self.region == AWS_REGIONS['N.Virginia']:
                s3.create_bucket(Bucket=self.name)
            else:
                s3.create_bucket(
                    Bucket=self.name,
                    CreateBucketConfiguration={'LocationConstraint': self.region})
        except ClientError as ex:
            if 'BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou' not in str(ex):
                raise
        sleep(1)
        wait.until(lambda: self.exists)
        self._bucket = s3.Bucket(self.name)
        self.bucket.wait_until_exists()
```

The `wait.until` call is a method that takes a lambda and returns when its value is positive.

The issue I encounter the most often is that after going through the `create` method, subsequent calls (such as `put_replication` and the likes) fail with a message indicating the bucket doesn't exist... and that, is very frustrating ;)

I have encountered the issue even with "sleep(2)" in both the create and delete methods.

issue_title: Add support for streaming (transfer-encoding: chunked) v4 uploads
issue_body:
While this issue will probably be resolved in botocore, I wanted to file it here as it seems issues filed against botocore don't get much attention.

The thrust of it is that AWS S3 supports streaming uploads with v4 signer (http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/API/sigv4-streaming.html), however, boto computed the entire content hash upfront. There is no way to opt into the streaming v4 upload.

For an upload that originates from another stream, this issue means buffering on disk or in memory to compute the content hash before uploading to S3. It'd be great to expose streaming v4 uploads to the consumers.

The issue against botocore is https://github.com/boto/botocore/issues/995

issue_title: zope.tal error with i18n:ignore
issue_body:
It gives me the following traceback:
```
      File "/home/gil/.buildout/eggs/Zope2-2.13.22-py2.7.egg/Products/Five/browser/pagetemplatefile.py", line 59, in __call__
        sourceAnnotations=getattr(debug_flags, 'sourceAnnotations', 0),
      File "/home/gil/.buildout/eggs/zope.pagetemplate-3.6.3-py2.7.egg/zope/pagetemplate/pagetemplate.py", line 126, in pt_render
        raise PTRuntimeError(str(self._v_errors))
       - Warning: Compilation failed
       - Warning: zope.tal.taldefs.I18NError: bad i18n attribute: u'ignore', at line 15, column 5
    PTRuntimeError: ['Compilation failed', "zope.tal.taldefs.I18NError: bad i18n attribute: u'ignore', at line 15, column 5"]
```

Seems that the problem comes from ``zope.tal`` (version 3.5.2) where only a [few specific attributes are allowed](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.tal/blob/3.5/src/zope/tal/taldefs.py#L58) and as ``ignore`` is not one of them it [raises an error](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.tal/blob/3.5/src/zope/tal/talgenerator.py#L501).

I will submit a pull request to zope.tal to allow it, we should probably warn in our docs about this?

issue_title: `gevent` integration does not work with `gevent.pool.Group.map`
issue_body:
The context is not properly passed from parent greenlet to child greenlet when using `gevent.pool.Group().map`. Spawning greenlets directly works:

```
pool = gevent.pool.Group()

# Context passes correctly
greenlets = [pool.spawn(myfunc, arg) for arg in args]
results = [g.get() for g in greenlets]

# Context does not pass correctly
results = pool.map(myfunc, args)
```

After some debugging, I tracked it to [this point in `ddtrace`](https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/blob/d2b1ef00597709876f3f479a31bbaf8e65663632/ddtrace/contrib/gevent/provider.py#L25-L26). 

Basically, before spawning a `TracedGreenlet`, it asks `gevent` for the current greenlet, and takes the context attached to that greenlet and attaches it to the new greenlet. However, when using `pool.map`, the "current" greenlet is not a greenlet; its an instance of [`IMap`](https://github.com/gevent/gevent/blob/f3acb176d0f0f1ac797b50e44a5e03726f687c53/src/gevent/pool.py#L181), which has not inherited the context.

issue_title: Cannot install editable package
issue_body:
I wanted to install a package on a local drive as an editable package under Windows:

    D:\projects\myproject>pipenv install -e D:\projects\DjangoApps\mypackage
  
##### Describe your environment

1. OS: Windows 10 Pro, 64 bit
1. Python version: Python 3.6.1
1. Pipenv version: pipenv, version 8.3.2

##### Expected result

the package is listed in Pipfile and `myproject` finds the `mypackage` dependency 

##### Actual result

The following error occurs:

    Installing -e D:\projects\DjangoApps\mypackage…
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\runpy.py", line 193, in _run_module_as_main
        "__main__", mod_spec)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\runpy.py", line 85, in _run_code
        exec(code, run_globals)
      File "C:\Program Files (x86)\Python36-32\Scripts\pipenv.exe\__main__.py", line 9, in <module>
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\vendor\click\core.py", line 722, in __call__
        return self.main(*args, **kwargs)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\vendor\click\core.py", line 697, in main
        rv = self.invoke(ctx)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\vendor\click\core.py", line 1066, in invoke
        return _process_result(sub_ctx.command.invoke(sub_ctx))
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\vendor\click\core.py", line 895, in invoke
        return ctx.invoke(self.callback, **ctx.params)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\vendor\click\core.py", line 535, in invoke
        return callback(*args, **kwargs)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\cli.py", line 1880, in install
        converted = convert_deps_from_pip(package_name)
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\site-packages\pipenv\utils.py", line 547, in convert_deps_from_pip
        os.path.isfile(req.name)) and not req.vcs:
      File "c:\program files (x86)\python36-32\lib\genericpath.py", line 30, in isfile
        st = os.stat(path)
    TypeError: stat: path should be string, bytes, os.PathLike or integer, not NoneType  

##### Solution

I checked the code in `pyenv/utils.py` and at the line 547, i.e. around:

    [...]
      if (req.uri or (os.path.isfile(req.path) if req.path else False) or
            os.path.isfile(req.name)) and not req.vcs:
    [...]

the error occurs because `req.name` is `None`. Hence the simple solution is check as for `req.path`:

    if (req.uri or (os.path.isfile(req.path) if req.path else False) or
            (os.path.isfile(req.name) if req.name else False)) and not req.vcs:

At least on my pc it worked. The Pipfile looks as follows after installing the editable package:

   [packages]
    ...
    django = "==1.10.5"
    "-e d:\\projects\\djangoapps\\mypackage" = "*"

issue_title: documentation for using using existing virtualenv?
issue_body:
I create my virtualenvs manually and use explicit paths in my projects rather than alter `PATH`.  I have an intense dislike of tools that require an altered environment or otherwise encourage cargo cult behaviour, so I have avoided `activate`, `virtualenvwrapper` and such things.

Initially I was turned off by pipenv's apparent insistence of creating virtualenvs for me and the complete inscrutability of how it does so.  The documentation seems to suggest that my only other option was a `.venv` in my project directory, which I also do not want.

I just now discovered via browsing the issues that pipenv will apparently honour `VIRTUAL_ENV`.  As far as I can tell, this variable is only used by other tools to detect an "activated" virtual env.  But virtualenvs don't need to be "activated" to be used; e.g. python, pip, and other tools work just fine by invoking their full path.  I had expected pipenv to behave similarly if installed into the virtualenv.

However, running with `VIRTUAL_ENV` produces (inconsistently) an annoying "courtesy notice", and this (and the function name `warn_in_virtualenv`) leads me to suspect that the scenario is somehow degenerate.

So, what's the story?  Is this feature supported and should it be documented?  What is the approprate procedure for managing my dependencies in an existing virtualenv?

If it is intended that pipenv be the recommended tool for managing project dependencies (which is why I am here) then I imagine it appropriate to be far more explicit about how it does things rather than performing magic on the user's behalf.  I also imagine it appropriate for distinct functionality to be less coupled, e.g. lockfile management from virtualenv creation.  Considering the lauded zen, one might suspect that python packaging is losing its buddha-nature.

issue_title: Documentation should describe what pipenv does and who it's for
issue_body:
Looking at [the docs](https://pipenv.readthedocs.io/en/latest/), we learn that:

* pipenv is "python dev workflow for humans" – that sounds good, I'm a human with a python dev workflow
* it's "the officially recommended Python packaging tool"
* it "bring[s] the best of all packaging worlds to the Python world"
* it solves "multi-faceted" problems
* it "replaces ... manual pip calls"

Basically what I get from this is that everyone should be using pipenv because it solves all the problems that other tools solved but better. What I *don't* get from it is any indication of what it actually does. This is especially confusing since AFAICT it doesn't actually solve any of my packaging problems.

[I asked about this on twitter](https://twitter.com/vorpalsmith/status/960054198757814273), and as far as I can tell the conclusion is that pipenv is targeted specifically at solving one specific problem in Python development: when you're working on an application you'll be deploying into a virtualenv (e.g. a django website), and want to control the dependencies used in the virtualenv, keep them consistent across your team, manage upgrades in a systematic way, etc.

I write Python libraries. I use pip and virtualenv and requirements.txt files a lot. For example:

* Creating ad-hoc virtualenvs to test particular combinations of python/library versions to reproduce reported issues
* Installing packages for general use into my user environment
* Recording which packages are needed to run tests, or build the docs, or format the source code, for convenience of other devs and for use in CI (e.g. `test-requirements.txt`, `rtd-requirements.txt`)

So far as I can tell, all of these are out-of-scope of pipenv, right? It's simply not intended at all to help developers like me? Or is it?

It would be very helpful if the docs could start with some information about who this project is intended for and what problems it's trying to help them solve.

issue_title: Race condition when VCS dependency has non-version-locked dependency
issue_body:
Conditions:
1. installing a dependency with a specific version, say `Django==1.8.18`
1. also installing a vcs dependency which itself depends on the same package, but an does not restrict the version, setup.py just includes `django`
1. Results in a race condition where the install of the package sometime installs the pinned version, 1.8.18, and sometimes installed the un-pinned, latest version, 1.11.10.

I have repeated it both locally and on AWS servers with the files below, though it happens intermittently locally and pretty reliably on AWS.

Pipfile
```
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"


[requires]

python_version = "2.7"


[dev-packages]


[packages]

django = "==1.8.18"
django-affect = { editable = true, git = "https://github.com/SummitESP/django-affect.git", ref = "1.5.0" }
```

Dockerfile
```
FROM python:2.7
RUN set -ex && pip install pipenv --upgrade
RUN set -ex && mkdir -p /srv/app
WORKDIR /srv/app
COPY Pipfile Pipfile
COPY Pipfile.lock Pipfile.lock
RUN set -ex && pipenv install --deploy --system --verbose
RUN pip freeze
```

issue_title: Consider support for file like objects instead of filenames
issue_body:
The API is currently designed in a way that it tightly coupled with the file system. The `parse` function for example expects the `Pipfile` to be a filename: https://github.com/pypa/pipfile/blob/master/pipfile/api.py#L70

It'd great if we could find a way to decouple the API from the filesystem, at least in parts.  This won't work for functions like `walk_up`, but when I look at `parse` it'd relatively easy to just pass in a file like object:

```
def parse(self, file_handle):
    # Open the Pipfile.
    content = f.read()
    ...
```

Same goes for the `find` function. Instead of calling `os.getcwd` explicitly in `find`, call it in `walk_up`:

```
 @staticmethod
def find(max_depth=3):
     """Returns the path of a Pipfile in parent directories."""
     i = 0
     for c, d, f in walk_up():
     ...
```

```
def walk_up(bottom):
    """mimic os.walk, but walk 'up' instead of down the directory tree.
    From: https://gist.github.com/zdavkeos/1098474
    """

    bottom = os.path.realpath(os.getcwd())
    ...
```

This would help external tools a lot to work with the API. There are probably still a lot of design decisions to make here, but if there's any interest in supporting external tools I'd be happy to submit a PR that addresses some of the points I mentioned earlier.

LINEBREAK_AT_THE_END: |

you will have json like this:

{
  "LINEBREAK_AT_THE_END": "Linebreak at the end\n",

  "NO_LINEBREAKS": "No linebreaks\n",
  "ZERO": "Zero"
}
```
Any suggestions on how to deal with this at least with some workaround?

issue_title: Prefixing `db_table` breaks models with long names.
issue_body:
Django will not allow a default `db_table` longer than the backend/connection allows. Fluent is explicitly assigning a `db_table` (to give it a prefix), which is disabling this truncation by Django.

I think Django should probably still truncate it to the max length allowed by the connection, even if it is explicitly set. Presumably there is no way for a table to exist in the database with a name that is longer than the max length.

However, it would take a while to get that change into Django and it probably wouldn't be backported to 1.7, 1.8, 1.9.

So, `django-fluent-contents` and `django-fluent-pages` should both do what Django does, and truncate the prefixed table name. Here are the offending lines:

https://github.com/edoburu/django-fluent-contents/blob/44154533c347b2985c28627cb15e4cdf7c441e76/fluent_contents/models/db.py#L177

and:

https://github.com/edoburu/django-fluent-pages/blob/master/fluent_pages/models/db.py#L55

And here's the Django code:

https://github.com/django/django/blob/7bb373e3097fe8e000e0bba005ff2dcfc18ab9a5/django/db/models/options.py#L214-L217

That Django code has been around for a long time, so it should work for all supported versions of Django, and probably several older versions too.

Although, as an user, it's much easier to squeeze the dimensions of my mask before calling clear_border()`, but I think the function should also be squeezing the dimensions, just to avoid any confusion. If that's a valid argument, I would be glad to submit a PR for the same.

issue_title: Set up automatic update of the dev docs
issue_body:
It would be really nice if someone could set up an automatic (per commit or nightly) build and push of the dev version of the scikit-image docs to http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/ .

The current version is at least two months outdated (could be much more ... I don't see a date or  git commit version) because it doesn't contain the new `skimage.restoration` sub-package added in #800 .

I think having online docs especially for new functionality is important, so that users can give feedback before the API is solidified by a stable release.

Maybe http://readthedocs.org or a `cron` job on `scikit-image.org` or `ci.scipy.org` could be used for this?

issue_title: Flattening histogram into a feature vector in scikit-image/skimage/feature/_hog.py is redundant.
issue_body:
In return statements of scikit-image/skimage/feature/_hog.py, ravel() is used to flatten histogram into a vector. It is redundant as last user can add ravel function if it is needed.

In my case, I needed histogram not flattened; so I had to modify in source code.

issue_title: skimage.external.tifffile is out-of-date
issue_body:
The version of `tifffile.py` included in `skimage.external` is slightly out-of-date with respect to the [version on the author's website](http://www.lfd.uci.edu/~gohlke/code/tifffile.py.html).

Critically, the version in scikit-image includes a typo in the [`_replace_by`](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/1b38c172800b45c04fcf958c4e8712ae7374ea8a/skimage/external/tifffile/tifffile_local.py#L3117) function, which prevents the C-extension code in `_tifffile.so` from getting imported.  Thus, although the `_tifffile.so` C-extension is built properly, it isn't ever used.

Fortunately, a pure-python implementation of `decodelzw()` exists as a fallback. Unfortunately, it is an order of magnitude slower than the C implementation.

issue_title: bootstrap_jquery_url template tag calls slim version of jQuery
issue_body:
The template tag `{% bootstrap_jquery_url %}` currently calls the **slim** version of jQuery, which doesn't support quite a few features of the full jQuery version. See here:

https://github.com/zostera/django-bootstrap4/blob/master/bootstrap4/bootstrap.py#L19

I'm happy to issue a P.R. to include the full version of jQuery, but it raises the question of which direction to head:

Option (1): make the existing template tag point to the full version.
Option (2): make the existing template tag point to the full version, and add another template tag called `{% bootstrap_jquery_slim_url %}`
Option (3): add a template tag called `{% bootstrap_jquery_full_url %}` that points to the full version.

`django-bootstrap3` currently points to the full version of jQuery.

Thoughts?

issue_title: search: elasticsearch schema fields wrongly mapped to numeric
issue_body:
The elasticsearch mapping for Record contains `numeric_detection": True`. This  enables the numeric fields autodetection.
With this option enabled, if a field which is not explicitly defined in the schema is present in a document and if it looks like a number, then it will mapped as a number. If next documents don't contain an integer for this field, the error `MapperParsingException` will be returned.

*Scenario:*
```
# create demo records
$ inveniomanage records create -t marcxml < invenio/testsuite/data/demo_record_marc_data.xml
# index the data
$ inveniomanage shell
  $ from invenio_records.models import Record
  $ from invenio.ext.es import index_record
  $ [index_record(r.id) for r in Record.query.all()]
...
RequestError: TransportError(400, u'MapperParsingException[failed to parse [universal_decimal_classification_number.universal_decimal_classification_number]]; nested: NumberFormatException[multiple points]; ')
```

issue_title: WebSearch: improve browsing in tiny collections
issue_body:
Originally

When browsing in a tiny collection, say one that contains only 1,000
records while the site as a whole contains 1,000,000 records, the
browse results pages usually do not return any values back and do not
even print some user-related hint as to what is happening.

We should do two things: (i) minimally, an indication to the user
should be printed saying that no values were found yet and offering
a link to continue browsing depending on the temporary term where the
previous browse attempt stopped; (ii) ideally, for tiny collections,
the collection-specific browsing should not be done_a posteriori_
by post-filtering of the any-collection values, but_a priori_ so
that if a collection contains only few thousands records (up to some
`CFG_WEBSEARCH_BROWSE_COLLECTION_POSTFILTERING_LIMIT` we shall
introduce), the browse engine could dynamically fetch all the values
for all the records in the given collection and browse inside this
list rather than using post-filtering of any-collection values.

issue_title: cli: create basic CLI API
issue_body:
Currently, records can be loaded to an Invenio 3 instance via shell commands like:

```console
$ echo '{"title": "My test title"}' | \
  invenio3 records create -i 56ed3d90-a566-11e5-afbb-28d2444be438
```

using CLI API of `invenio-records`.

However, the association between record UUIDs and record PIDs have to be done in Python, because `invenio-pidstore` does not have any CLI API.  

For the sake of homogeneous [record loading process](http://invenio.readthedocs.org/en/latest/installation/installation-detailed.html#invenio), it may be useful to create a basic CLI API to PID store, with a subset of some useful options such as:

```console
$ invenio3 pid get rec 56ed3d90-a566-11e5-afbb-28d2444be438
1
$ invenio3 pid create rec 2 40cc0f90-a567-11e5-b515-28d2444be438 REGISTERED
```

Alternatively, users could `curl` given records to `invenio-record-rest` APIs...  However a basic CLI API for PID store may be useful for other house-keeping purposes like:

- what PIDs do I have for record UUID?
- what PIDs are in RESERVED status?
- which objects are minted with this DOI provider?

etc.

issue_title: BTrees should support the PURE_PYTHON environment variable at runtime
issue_body:
This has become common for zopefoundation packages such as [persistent](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/blob/master/persistent/__init__.py#L21), [Acquisition](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Acquisition/blob/master/src/Acquisition/__init__.py#L945), [zope.security](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.security/blob/master/src/zope/security/proxy.py#L383) and so on. It's convenient for debugging and exploring the system, and also for testing.

BTrees does *not* support this variable, and this causes problems because it conflicts with `persistent`, which does:
```console
$ pip freeze | egrep  "BTree|pers"
BTrees==4.4.1

cryptography is an essential module in my kivy / twisted / pyOpenSSL based app.
Building finally succeeded (it wasn't straightforward) but now we are run time testing.
Thanks to a tip from "tshirtman" I had a meaningful rpdb interactive session as follows:

issue_title: Encoding formats
issue_body:
I'm not extremely familiar with the internals of Brotli or compression in general, so this may be a naive suggestion, but intuitively I think it aligns philosophically with Brotli's use of a built-in dictionary. Would it be practical for Brotli to detect strings of formats like base64 and hex and decode them to binary before compressing / re-encode them as part of the decompression process?

I noticed just now during a comparison between compressing a binary file and the equivalent base64 (https://github.com/kripken/emscripten/issues/5104#issuecomment-302939570) that the base64 version ended up a bit larger, which it occurs to me shouldn't need to be the case given that the two contain almost exactly the same information.

issue_title: Problems installing Python bindings on Mac
issue_body:
Hello! I'm having some trouble installing the Python bindings on Mac OS, and just thought I'd mention it here in case anyone had similar trouble. This is within an Anaconda environment, Python 3.

Move into directory and install:

    $ cd /path/to/sentencepiece
    $ ./autogen.sh
    $ ./configure
    $ make
    $ make install

Success. Try to install Python bindings:

    $ python setup.py build
    Package sentencepiece was not found in the pkg-config search path.
    Perhaps you should add the directory containing `sentencepiece.pc'
    to the PKG_CONFIG_PATH environment variable
    No package 'sentencepiece' found
    Failed to find sentencepiece pkgconfig

Fix the path.

    $ export PKG_CONFIG_PATH=`pwd`/..

And try again:

    $ python setup.py build
    running build
    running build_py
    running build_ext
    building '_sentencepiece' extension
    gcc -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include/python3.6m -c sentencepiece_wrap.cxx -o build/temp.macosx-10.7-x86_64-3.6/sentencepiece_wrap.o -std=c++11 -g -O2 -I/Users/neubig/usr/include
    In file included from sentencepiece_wrap.cxx:3124:
    /Users/neubig/usr/include/sentencepiece_processor.h:141:8: error: no template named 'unique_ptr' in namespace 'std'
      std::unique_ptr<Rep> rep_;
      ~~~~~^
    /Users/neubig/usr/include/sentencepiece_processor.h:181:8: error: no template named 'unique_ptr' in namespace 'std'
      std::unique_ptr<std::string> rep_;
      ~~~~~^    
    ...

The strange thing is that when I run the command on its own by copy-pasting things seem to work.

    $ gcc -Wno-unused-result -Wsign-compare -Wunreachable-code -DNDEBUG -g -fwrapv -O3 -Wall -Wstrict-prototypes -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include -arch x86_64 -I/anaconda3/envs/python3/include/python3.6m -c sentencepiece_wrap.cxx -o build/temp.macosx-10.7-x86_64-3.6/sentencepiece_wrap.o -std=c++11 -g -O2 -I/Users/neubig/usr/include

### Reproduction Steps

This only displays the column names (and filter buttons) in the widget:
```
import pandas
df=pandas.DataFrame({"mobs":[1,2,3,4]})
import qgrid
qgrid.set_defaults(grid_options={
            'fullWidthRows': True,
            'syncColumnCellResize': True,
            'forceFitColumns': True,
            'rowHeight': 28,
            'enableColumnReorder': False,
            'enableTextSelectionOnCells': True,
            'editable': True,
            'autoEdit': False
        })
qgrid.show_grid(df)
```

----

This displays the column data as it should be displayed:
```
import pandas
df=pandas.DataFrame({"mobs":[1,2,3,4]})
import qgrid
qgrid.set_defaults(grid_options=None)
qgrid.show_grid(df)
```

issue_title: Deprecate things we nominally got rid of in v1.1
issue_body:
In version 1.1, we semi-deprecated a lot of old syntax in preference to our new API.  However, we didn't officially deprecate any (or at least much) of the old API.  I think it is time to do so.

For the next release, v1.3, I'd like to have all the old syntax give deprecation warnings (not errors yet) indicating what the preferred function to use is.

issue_title: Population section radial distance inconsistency
issue_body:
The section radial distance as calculated from a population seems inconsistent with that of individual neurons. This can be tested with a small sample of synthesized morphologies, where the soma positions of each cell differ:

```
import numpy as np
import neurom as nm
from neurom.io.utils import get_morph_files

DIR = 'path/to/some/morphologies'

pop = nm.load_neurons(DIR)
nrns = [nm.load_neuron(f) for f in get_morph_files(DIR)]

rad_dist_pop = nm.get('section_radial_distances', pop)

rad_dist_nrns = []
for nrn in nrns:
    rad_dist_nrns.extend( nm.get('section_radial_distances', nrn))

rad_dist_nrns = np.array(rad_dist_nrns)

print np.alltrue(rad_dist_pop == rad_dist_nrns)
```

Expected output: `True`, observed output: `False`.

issue_title: Thoughts on design
issue_body:
I would like to discuss some things that I have noticed in NeuroM. The suggestion that I present requires a lot of changes in order to be applied, thus I would like make clear that this post is more on thinking into the future than making any changes in the current structure of NeuroM. Let's begin:

We have adopted a tree representation of our data which is used throughout the modules via iterators, maps etc. 

I understand that in languages where the trees are expressed with pointers, creating iterators and recursively generating stuff is super fast. In python however, generators are not fast, and functional programming will always lag behind the not so cheap function calls (although as far as we do not put them in loops, it's not that bad).

Iterators in Python are memory efficient because they do not store everything in memory at once when iterative calculations are yielded, but in our case that we first create the tree in memory and then return the parent node, we are in a lose lose situation. Plus, we always need to apply an O(n) traversal for operations where this could be avoided by a lookup table structure.

Our loaders and processing are slow when the order of magnitude of number of cells is hundred and up, needing a lot of minutes to load or process a population. When I tried to profile and optimize the loading I stumbled upon the scary truth: Our bottleneck is mainly the make_tree function which iteratively generates a tree from the data. Unfortunately, the recursive alternative is even worse due to the unoptimized recursion in Python.

I also noticed that in a lot of functions we convert the tree to a list of elements (```as_elements```), and I am wondering what would be the ratio of the times that we need a tree as a tree over the times that we need it as a array of points. Maybe we would need to write less code if we had the points to begin with.

Being stuck on the python level, we will always find these overheads in front of us, which kills the performance of NeuroM and causes serious problems when populations need to be analyzed.

You will ask: what alternatives do we have? Well, we can always go full numpy and exploit the speed of light (c). Instead of creating trees we can load the data through numpy, and generate a connectivity matrix for the "parenting" (I think that Pneumatk uses this design, not sure though). I suspect that this will eliminate the majority of the overheads.

What do you think?

Any website or mobile application that would like to add the threema icon in reference to the [threema app](threema.ch). Case in point: We would like to add a share button with the threema icon on our web page.

- [ ] This is a single icon or matched pair (Ex: `lock` / `unlock`)

- [ ] I have [searched for existing issues](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues) and to the best of my knowledge this is not a duplicate
- [ ] I have included at least one example image if it could be helpful (optional)

issue_title: add ability to have custom postgresql.conf. pg_hba.conf
issue_body:
Add the following lines to patroni.yml:

postgresql_config_file: /path/to/custom/file
pg_hba_file: /path/to/custom/file

If present, these configuration variables will cause the files named to be copied to the data directory after it is initialized and before PostgreSQL is started up.

A TODO for me.

issue_title: Run pg_rewind for the former master that was shut down uncleanly (i.e. killed -9)
issue_body:
Currently, pg_rewind can only process the former master being shut down cleanly. This is useful for the case of split brain/connection loss between the former master and DCS, but In real life some of the shutdowns will happen because of the PostgreSQL crash, due to bad hardware or bugs.

What we could do is to start the node that crashed in a single user mode and issue a checkpoint before trying to do pg_rewind on it. We have to check, though, that no data loss occurs as a result.

issue_title: path formatted parameters
issue_body:
### Description
Hi - I'm trying to build an api that allows extensions to be added to the API created. For example:

`/v1/service/{serviceid}/{extension}`

Where the method invoked would pass {extension} as the remainder of the URL as a parameter to the invoked code, allowing it to process that part of the URL. 

I understand and have tested in Flask something like this:
`@app.route('/myapp/<serviceId>/<path:extension>', methods = ['POST', 'GET'])`

When I post something like:
`/service/myservice/my/custom/extension`
and as expected get serviceId='myservice' as one parameter and the remainder of the url (e.g. 'my/custom/extension' ) as the value for extension. 

I built what I believe to be equivalent using OpenAPI:
```
paths:
  /service/{serviceid}/{extension}:
    post:
      tags:
      - "serviceextension"
      summary: "Extension endpoint for services""
      operationId: "my_extension_service_by_id"
      produces:
      - "application/json"
      parameters:
      - name: 'serviceid'
        in: path
        type: string
        required: true
      - name: 'extension'
        in: path
        type: string
        required: true
        format: path

```
### Expected behaviour

I expect when posting an URL:

`/service/myservice/my/custom/extension`

I would have the my_extension_service_by_id invoked with  serviceid='myservice' and extension='my/custom/extension'

### Actual behaviour

I get a 404 error

### Steps to reproduce

Create an API as above - I used http://editor.swagger.io/#/ and generated the server stubs using python-flask

### Additional info:
python --version
Python 3.6.0b2

pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"
Version: 1.0.129
Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`

issue_title: How do I access Flask current_app?
issue_body:
```
server.py

#!/usr/bin/env python3

from connexion.resolver import RestyResolver
import connexion

def create_app():
    app = connexion.FlaskApp(__name__, specification_dir='swagger/')
    app.add_api('mapmybook.yaml',validate_responses=True, strict_validation=True, resolver=RestyResolver('api'))

    return app

if __name__ == '__main__':
    create_app().run(port=9090)
```

```
api/routes.py

from flask import current_app
def get() -> list:
    print(current_app)
    return items
```
results in `RuntimeError: Working outside of application context.`
I'm using Flask 1.0 with connexion 1.4.2, creating the app as seen above. Is there a way to access Flask's `current_app` from other modules?
I keep getting `RuntimeError: Working outside of application context.` when calling current_app in API modules.

issue_title: sqlalchemy example return 500 on missing pet id (instead of 404)
issue_body:
### Description
The sqlalchemy example 


### Expected behaviour
Missing pet should return 404.


### Actual behaviour
API returns a 500


### Steps to reproduce
```
cd connexion/examples/sqlalchemy
python app.py
curl -X GET --header 'Accept: application/json' 'http://localhost:8080/pets/3'
```

### Additional info:
#### Fix:
https://github.com/zalando/connexion/compare/master...dtkav:sqlalchemy_example_bugfix?expand=1

issue_title: Subsetting by columns fails with one share
issue_body:
Don't know if this is expected behavior or not (it wasn't to me).

In short,if you want to get one share:
```
foo = Quandl.get('GOOG/NYSE_AAPL') 
```

works, but if you want to get one column from one share
```
foo = Quandl.get('GOOG/NYSE_AAPL.4')
```
doesn't.  

You can get single columns from multiple shares (as on the python demo page): 
```
 foo = Quandl.get(['GOOG/NYSE_AAPL.4','GOOG/NASDAQ_MSFT.4']) 
```

And you can also get a column from one share by making it a one-item list:
```
foo = Quandl.get(['GOOG/NYSE_AAPL.4'])
```
so it's not a show-stopper, but rather a failure of expectations about the way the "retrieve just one column" thing works.

--------------

And while I'm on the subject, Python uses 0-indexed arrays.  The fourth column of a Python array bar is 
```
bar[3]
```
which causes a tiny bit of mental dissonance when I have to type ".4" to fetch the fourth column from the Quandl server in Python.

issue_title: models with ID columns not named ID cause exception
issue_body:
With a model that has an ID column not named ID, and the following code:

```python
class TestView(PandasView):
    queryset = WeirdIDModel.objects.all()
    serializer_class = WeirdIDModelSerializer
 ```
when trying to query the class, you get an error KeyError, "id".

Reading that stack trace and the source I see that this is because by default we run "set_index('id') on the df, and this can be fixed in three obvious ways:

1. set pandas_index on the meta of the serializer
2. override get_index_fields on the serializer
3. override get_dataframe
```python
```
1. setting the index seems to be needless duplication (I've already written once what the index of this model is)
2. an override to get_index_fields I think would just create behaviour that should be default:
         WeirdIDModelSerializer._meta.pk.name gives the ID, for my version of Django at least

so something like 
```python
try:
    id_name = (self.<field for model>)._meta.pk.name
except:
    id_name = 'id'
default_fields = [id_name]
```

3. Thinking about it more, and reading the source, it seems odd to even set an index, especially without preserving the original column - I think that DRF classes that we're mimicking would by default pass the index through to the client, and since I'm trying to drop-in replace some existing code which might rely on that, I'll have to replicate that manually.

issue_title: state.generate_testcase() broken
issue_body:
`state.generate_testcase` has a typo which causes it to break. it publishes the wrong event, `will_generate_inputs`, instead of the existing `will_generate_testcase`.

https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/36a58810f905e299ff63a6208dc4e08eeb891869/manticore/core/state.py#L375

The fix for this is trivial, we just need to correct the string, but it's concerning that this bug was able to slip by without triggering test failures, or anything. To correct against these errors, we should probably declare these event key strings as constants somewhere and use those instead of literal strings.

issue_title: Require specification of the variable that forward/backward computation should target
issue_body: Currently, cnn calculates forward values and backward derivatives with respect to the last variable in the computation graph. This change should make it necessary for the user to explicitly choose the variable that is used as the target of forward, or start of backward computation.

issue_title: Incorrect range when reading files with variable gate spacing using read_rsl
issue_body:
When a file in which some rays do not have an identical gate spacing as the first ray of the first sweep the range attribute of the created **Radar** object does not contain the correct gate locations for all rays but rather the ranges for the first ray.  Currently the CfRadial convention on which the **Radar** class is based has not manner for expressing a radar volume in which the gate locations vary by ray.  Until a preferred method of expressing such volumes is determine it would be reasonable for Py-ART to raise a error or warning when data in this form is encountered.

This issue was reported by Anderson Gama.

issue_title: DISCUSSION: abstractions of service calls in storage
issue_body:
Generally speaking, the library functions seem to abstract when service interactions occur from the user.  In other words, some operations are local to the object (e.g [acl.grant](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/storage/acl.py#L125)), others interact with the service (e.g. [acl.save](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/storage/acl.py#L414)), while still others interact only some of the time (e.g. [bucket.default_object_acl](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/storage/bucket.py#L124)).
If you're going to follow this model (and I'm not sure you should), I think you need to add preconditions to most service calls to ensure that confusing things don't happen. 
A good example of this would be the save and reload functions in the ACL class.  When should the user call reload()?  I don't think it's clear.  What happens when the user calls save() but the target ACL has changed on the service side since we last loaded it?  

This abstraction is especially dangerous because it works very well when a single client is involved but creates subtle problems when deployed at scale.

/cc @thobrla

issue_title: pantsd memory leak when used with v2 changed
issue_body: based on recent testing, it seems like the memory leak we saw before when fully priming a pre-fork `BuildGraph` is back when the daemon is used with v2 changed. it does not manifest with e.g. repeated `list ::`. I think this points to #4424 and the inherent non-daemon-safety of `BuildGraph` + `Target` as the culprit.

issue_title: cache keys not matching for remote cache with v2 engine enabled
issue_body:
I'm running pants 1.3.0rc2.  As far as I know, this was an issue on rc1 and rc0 as well.  We have two continuous integration machines (Centos 7.3.1611) that run the build with every commit.  Our developer machines are running osx.  I started noticing that the developer machines were not finding any hits from the cache created on the build machines.  All zinc entries would miss.  Also, entries for one of our custom tasks would miss.  The debug logs showed that the requests to the cache were for keys that didn't exist in the cache and 404s were returned.

The cache did work between the two build machines.  One of the build machines would find hits for artifacts created on the other machine and vice-versa.  It was only the developer machines missing.

I first did a comparison of options between the build options on the build machines and developer machine.  I found no significant differences, only things like python path.  I also used the same pants.ini file on the developer machine and still got misses.

I wrote a task to dump out `target.invalidation_hash(None)` on a small target and ran this on both machines.  This hash differs.  If I dump all fields of the payload of the target, I see that `field.sources.files_hash` differs for the `sources` field of the target.

I used python to compute the sha1 of the files and their paths.  These all match, but the `invalidation_hash` is still different.  On both machines the manually computed hash is different from `invalidation_hash`.

When I switch to the old engine, invalidation_hash matches on the machines, and also matches my manually computed version of the hash.  The cache works normally between machines (mostly hits) when I switch to the old engine.

I will attach the source of my task that dumps info about the targets and the output of this on both machines.

In addition the integ tests don't catch the issue because they test bootstrapping but not calling a repl.  Also because of the way this is bootstrapped the repl-spec should be removed because it will not behave the way the user expects.  This is for similar reasons to scala-compiler.

issue_title: Add attribute to junit_tests to allow sharding tests into separate JVMs?
issue_body:
I had a unit test for some Spark integration code where Spark corrupted some shared global state used by our Hadoop "mini cluster" unit tests, causing those other tests to fail when run in the same JVM as the Spark unit test. Our temporary solution was to add a dummy extra environment variable to the applicable `junit_tests` target via the `extra_env_vars` attribute to force Pants to run that test target in its own JVM.

This seems like a hack though because, while `extra_env_vars` has the side effect of running tests in a separate JVM from other targets not sharing the same `extra_env_vars`, this is not the advertised purpose of `extra_env_vars` (or any other attribute on `junit_tests` for that matter).

I would like to submit a PR adding a new string attribute to the `junit_tests` for the purpose of making the separate JVM requirement explicit. This new attribute would become part of the partitioning logic in the `_partition` method in [src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/junit_run.py](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/junit_run.py). Thus, if a `junit_tests` target had a `jvm_tag` attribute, it would be guaranteed to run in a JVM with only other targets annotated with the same `jvm_tag` string value.

This attribute could be called `jvm_tag`, although this doesn't seem like the best name possible.

Any objections? Comments?

issue_title: Shade tool classpaths
issue_body:
Given a main class and a classpath we should be able to auto-shade tool classpaths.

This feature prevents jvm tool classpaths from leaking, allowing a pants jvm tool to work on all underlying code.  An example where we can break today is in the junit runner which uses guava.  The junit runner guava version can conflict with tests being run today but it should never interfere.

The new tooling might use [jarjar](https://code.google.com/p/jarjar/).
The new tooling should cache/invalidate results - only run the shading once per tool classpath fingerprint.

issue_title: allow idea-plugin to override exporting sources and docs
issue_body:
Currently, `./pants help export` displays the following options:
```
--[no-]export-libraries-javadocs (default: False)
    Causes libraries with javadocs to be output.
--[no-]export-libraries-sources (default: False)
    Causes libraries with sources to be output.
```

These are essentially switched on when `idea-plugin` is invoked, which leads to a resolve error if the sources or docs jars are not available. Although they can be turned off by checkboxes in the GUI import dialog in the Pants IntelliJ plugin, it would be great if these could be turned off with options in the `IdeaPluginGen` task, so that a user can open their pants project in IntelliJ, from the command line, even if not all the sources or docs jars are available for their project's JVM dependencies.

issue_title: Improve `CollectionOptionHander`'s ergonomics
issue_body:
Right now it has un-intuitive splitting semantics inherited from `String.split`, for example. 
```console
scala> import java.lang.String
import java.lang.String

scala> Seq(new String(",").split(","):_*)
res8: Seq[java.lang.String] = List()

scala> Seq(new String(",").split(", "):_*)
res9: Seq[java.lang.String] = List(,)

scala> Seq(new String(",").split(",a"):_*)
res10: Seq[java.lang.String] = List(,)

scala> Seq(new String(",").split(" ,"):_*)
res11: Seq[java.lang.String] = List(,)

scala> Seq(new String(",").split("b,"):_*)
res12: Seq[java.lang.String] = List(,)
```

It probably makes sense to ditch the `DelimitedOptionHandler` base class and instead just use a default `com.google.common.base.Splitter` but allowing for a custom one.

issue_title: Add native support for collections
issue_body:
Currently, in order to distinguish a "`list` of `Path`", we need to create a concrete type (`Paths`, in this case) and a function to execute the merge. Aside from being boilerplatey, it's also less efficient: we would never want to hold the intermediate portions of a nested aggregation in memory, and having intermediate nodes forces us to.

We will likely want to use "synthetic" types for this: see the synthetic [Sources](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/5d14bb156b78873734687fc975bf589f8af25c73/src/python/pants/engine/exp/examples/planners.py#L110) class (note: very old) that used to exist in the engine. It creates a pickleable, stable synthetic class wrapping some other value. In this case, you could imagine a `Collection(Path)` synthetic type, for example.

issue_title: context manager to temporarily adapt `sys.path`
issue_body:
For several projects we want to be able to import custom code not within a python package. The most convenient (and portable) way to this seems to be via a context manager, adapting `sys.path` appropriately:
```python
@contextmanager
def with_sys_path(d):
    p = d.as_posix()
    sys.path.append(p)
    yield
    if sys.path[-1] == p:
        sys.path.pop()
```
Alternatively, the complete functionality, i.e. importing a module by filesystem path could be provided:
```python
        with with_sys_path(path.parent):
            return import_module(path.name)
```

issue_title: Codedeploy Service Role Insufficent to invoke hooks
issue_body:
When using deployment preference settings, a service role for codedeploy is created with the following managed policy: arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSCodeDeployRoleForLambda

Deployments that invoke hook functions do not work unless you specify the FunctionName, because the above policy contains the following:

```
{
            "Action": [
                "lambda:InvokeFunction"
            ],
            "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:region:*:function:CodeDeployHook_*",
            "Effect": "Allow"
        }
```

Without a FunctionName set, the name is formed from the stack and resource name. 

If an org has naming standards, it is not desirable to hardcode function names. It would be better if you could pass in a pre-existing codedeploy role within the deployment preferences.

issue_title: @_MetricWrapper breaks pylint argument checks
issue_body:
`@_MetricWrapper` changes the call signature of the classes it wraps, breaking pylint argument checks. This causes us to have to annotate every single prometheus object with `# pylint: disable-msg=no-value-for-parameter` to avoid erroneous lint warnings. This gets rather silly.

If the mandatory arguments in the call signature of the wrapper and the metrics classes (like Histogram, Counter, etc) matched, pylint would no longer raise this warning.

Code pointers:
Wrapper: https://github.com/prometheus/client_python/blob/d858f0d39536c2fe10b78d6e26109272b6b2ea46/prometheus_client/core.py#L544
Histogram `__init__()`: https://github.com/prometheus/client_python/blob/d858f0d39536c2fe10b78d6e26109272b6b2ea46/prometheus_client/core.py#L847

issue_title: Fair rounding when converting floats to ints
issue_body:
When converting from floats to ints during saving an image, imageio always rounds down to the next-smallest integer value, instead of rounding to the closest one. e.g. a value of 245.99 will get rounded down to 245. Arguably, rounding up would lead to a better uint8 representation, and I'd suggest changing imageio's rounding behavior in [this function](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/blob/master/imageio/core/util.py#L57) accordingly.

Note that this is likely related to #289 , as `scipy.misc.imsave` (which is now deprecated and suggests using `imageio.imwrite`) rounds to the closest pixel value, so that `imsave` and `imwrite` will often produce different images: many pixels in the output differ by 1 intensity value. While not visually noticeable, this produced weird artefacts in my image analysis pipeline (which is how I found out about this in the first place).

issue_title: bigtiff isn't working (with fix)
issue_body:
It appears that an argument (append) was added to TiffWriter.__init__, but not the call in TiffFormat.Writer._open.

Original (in imageio/plugins/tifffile.py):
`
           self._tf = _tifffile.TiffWriter(self.request.get_local_filename(),
                                            bigtiff, byteorder, software)
`
Corrected:
`
           self._tf = _tifffile.TiffWriter(self.request.get_local_filename(),
                                            False, bigtiff, byteorder, software)
`

issue_title: Lint for unused translations
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

There are some strings in the codebase that are never referenced. 

### Expected behavior

Warn about unused translations

### User-facing consequences

Wastes time for translators.

I was thinking we can search if the string is referenced inside the `registerFoundMessages` function. The typical case of `$tr('translation'...` is easy to search for but sometimes translations are not referenced like that. Sometimes there is a map of translations that is passed into a function. Or in places like the Perseus exercise renderer, the strings are never actually referenced in our codebase. Thoughts @rtibbles?

issue_title: Username validation accepts hyphens ('-') when it shouldn't
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Josh in the Discourse forum ran into problems because his superuser username had a dash.

https://community.learningequality.org/t/kolibri-setup-issue/426/3

Confirmed that the setup wizard (and probably everything else using the username validation) accepts usernames with hyphens, which leads to a 400 error when creating a new user.

@radinamatic 

### Expected behavior

The setup wizard does not allow usernames with hyphens in them. Also, it would be nicer if this error was handled inside of the Setup Wizard step instead of showing a generic "something went wrong" error screen to provide some hints as to what happened.

### User-facing consequences

More users might run into this, especially since the invalid username text only appears when input is "invalid".


### Steps to reproduce

1. In the setup wizard try to create a superuser with a '-' in the username
1. Submit it and see the error screen

### Context
 * Kolibri version: 0.7
 * Operating system: any
 * Browser: any

issue_title: @cached mechanism broken in bionic (python 3.6)
issue_body:
Hi,

The `@cached` mechanism in `core/hookenv.py` is broken. The key for the `cache` dict is created as follows (from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/hookenv.py#L70) : 

```
key = str((func, args, kwargs))
```

But there's a subtle difference in how `str()` called on a dict (`kwargs`, here) behaves between xenial and bionic.

On xenial : 
```
 >>> a = {'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}
>>> str(a)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> b = {'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}
>>> str(b)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> str(a) == str(b)
True
```

On bionic : 
```
>>> a = {'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}
>>> str(a)
"{'rid': 'general-info:2', 'unit': 'ubuntu/0'}"
>>> b = {'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}
>>> str(b)
"{'unit': 'ubuntu/0', 'rid': 'general-info:2'}"
>>> str(a) == str(b)
False
```

In other words, on xenial, the orders of the keys in the dict doesn't matter, but on bionic it does.

So for example, the `relation_get()` method being cached, if I call `relation_get(rid=foo, unit=bar)` and `relation_get(unit=bar, rid=foo)`, on xenial they will have the same cache key, but on bionic they won't.

This shouldn't result in bugs however, just in slower calls. There is a twist however : some functions modify the object returned by the cached function call, which modify the object in the cache itself.

For example : 
```
@cached
def relation_for_unit(unit=None, rid=None):
    """Get the json represenation of a unit's relation"""
    unit = unit or remote_unit()
    relation = relation_get(unit=unit, rid=rid)
    for key in relation:
        if key.endswith('-list'):
            relation[key] = relation[key].split()
    relation['__unit__'] = unit
return relation
```
(taken from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/hookenv.py#L466)

This will add a `__unit___` key to the dict that is stored in the cache.

Now consider the following call, taken from https://github.com/juju/charm-helpers/blob/master/charmhelpers/core/services/helpers.py#L119 :
```
reldata = hookenv.relation_get(rid=rid, unit=unit)
```

On xenial, because `str(a) == str(b)` (see the first block of code), this call and `relation_get(unit=unit, rid=rid)` (kwargs in different order) will have the same key in the cache, and so will use the same cached object, containing `__unit__`. However on bionic, because `str(a)!=str(b)`, these 2 calls will not have the same key, and so will not use the same object from the cache. So the `reldata` object above will _never_ have a `__unit__` key in it.

The short term fix I'm going to propose here is to have all calls to `relation_get()` in charmhelpers have the same order for kwargs.

For the long term, this caching technique must be changed. Perhaps using https://docs.python.org/dev/library/functools.html#functools.lru_cache ? I'll leave that up to you.

There are several system tests for NI-DMM that are actually testing the code-generator. They were written before we created NI-FAKE.

#183 should be closed before this one is tackled.
